introduction: Inflammation of the vagina and vulva, usually evolving from vaginal discharge and secondary external irritation. Candida albicans is the commonest cause of candidal vulvo-vaginitis; Candida glabrata has also been identified. Candidal vaginitis is most common in: Pregnancy, Patients with diabetes mellitus, Those on long-term antibiotic therapy or oral contraceptives, Conditions associated with immune suppression, Corticosteroid use. Usually not acquired through sexual intercourse. Because of the close proximity between the anus and female genitalia, re-infections may occur from the gastrointestinal tract.

features: Up to 20% of women with the infection may be asymptomatic
If symptoms occur, they usually consist of vulval itching, soreness and a non-offensive vaginal discharge which may be curdy
Vulval erythema (redness) or excoriations from scratching
Erosions and crusting on the adjacent intertriginous skin
A minority of male partners may have balanitis, which is characterized by erythema of the glans penis or inflammation of the glans penis and foreskin (balanoposthitis)

drug: Clotrimazole 1% vaginal cream: Insert 5 g at night as a single dose; may be repeated once if necessary
Miconazole 2% intravaginal creamInsert 5 g applicator once daily for 10 - 14 days or twice daily for 7 days
Clotrimazole 500 mg intravaginally, as a single dose
Fluconazole 150 mg orally, as a single dose
Clotrimazole 1% cream apply twice daily for 7 days (for balanoposthitis)
Miconazole 2% cream twice daily for 7 days (for balanoposthitis)
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Fluconazole: Caution in patients with renal impairment
Fluconazole: Avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding
Fluconazole: Discontinue if signs or symptoms of hepatic disease develop (risk of hepatic necrosis)
Fluconazole: May cause nausea, abdominal discomfort, diarrhoea, flatulence, headache, skin rash and Steven-Johnson syndrome
Fluconazole: Discontinue treatment or monitor closely if infection is invasive or systemic)

differential_diagnoses: Other causes of vaginal discharge: see Gonorrhoea in women
complications: Emotional problems because of the recurrent nature of the infection, and dyspareunia
Very serious emotional problems in a non-sexually active person wrongly "accused" by parents, spouse or health care providers
prevention: After defecation cleaning should be done backwards to prevent faecal contamination of the vulva and vagina
